string.text: {=jPGOlC7O}I don't mind talking about it.

{=3yK4uXyt}I'm an engineer. Most of us learned our trade and got our contacts through campaigning in armies. It was a good system: it was the king's money, so the king's servants kept a close eye on us. But as everyone knows, kings on campaign eventually run out of money. The lords are in charge of fortifications now, and all they have to go on is the say-so of other engineers.

{=OYOwasMe}You can imagine - it's a bit of a racket. People build arches that fall down, and his colleagues go on recommending him because he's someone's brother or old apprentice. The greatest sin in our brotherhood is criticizing the work of other engineers.
{=JfTTbPag}Well, I don't play that game. If something's going to fall down and kill someone, I'll point that out. So, naturally, all my 'brothers' cut me out of contracts.
{=R36Bl55h}Most noble of you, sir.
{=oXnqNsvz}A man of integrity, or simply one who backbites his peers? I have no idea, and wouldn't want to take the risk of hiring you.

{=b1sCl4SI}So, anyway, I am forced to spend my time in taverns, taking whatever work can be found. I can oversee the building of most structures, or, if the need is there, I am also adept at knocking them down with catapults, rams, and mines.
{=RZizPvaI}I'm an engineer, with army training. I'm sure by now you've met a few of us around, and know a bit about what we can do.
{=836klxRM}You want to know about me? Well, that's a story, that's a story.
{=ivz2U3e2}See, there's a lot of tales about me, and only some of them are true. The Saracen call me by one name, the Moors by another.

{=0ty7hiZI} I've been in a lot of fights and I've killed a lot of people. Ask you around - you shouldn't just take my word for it. But never one who didn't deserve it, and never once outside a fair fight.

{=waUxgr5b}Let me tell you something else - war is not a game. It's not a game. Play by the rules and you die. The streets aren't the arena. If you think someone's your enemy, you take them down - don't wait for a polite challenge.
{=8duSHfDa}Ah, yes. Wise words. Do go on.
{=oyHzM0jb}You've said nothing about yourself, and your one word contradicts the other. I have better things to do.

{=1ubcomnD}I don't like to brag about my own deeds, so I'll leave it at that. I do a lot of work for the merchants around here, but they don't own me. I'm my own man. I just take their money. It's not like I'm a mercenary or something like that.
{=zXJvWvD5}I'm a warrior. A soldier of fortune, you might say. A damn good one, too, as my enemies would tell you if they could speak from the other world.
{=AT7pJ6Rm}It is not without some reluctance that I tell this story, for it hardly does honor to my name.
{=3J76GzZh}My father served as knight in a retinue in the east. We had a small estate to pay for his horse and armor. I was raised to take his place on the cavalry rosters when he came of age.
{=fwYGme3B} But the Saracens overran us, and he was slain, and our land was taken and its revenues now no doubt line the pockets of some duke.

{=4UMf7HDe}You may call it pride, but I do not wish to enlist as a foot soldier. So I work here in town, for merchants, for pay, a hired sword. All I can say is that I hope one day to purchase the land I need to serve our king, whoever sits on the throne, as my father did.
{=KtN79I0H}That's it? The dishonorable part? That you work for merchants? I'd thought it would be a lot worse.
{=902xqUja}A cavalier errands for traders! The shame of it!
{=wiR9bSfw}I try at least to pick and choose my master, and hope at least that some of our knightly honor rubs off on the men of commerce.

{=ammEPFoq}I'm a knight, formerly in the retinue of the lords of Casa de Urgell. Our skills, and our honor, needs no further introduction. But I was unable to afford my horse and armor, so I took work with merchants, as a hired sword.
{=B3UQTCRE}Refill my glass and I'll tell you.

{=H7tFXLVN}I'm a soldier by trade. I've served the Crown of Aragon for 20 years. Once upon a time they were worthy of being called 'armies.' We served together for years, and we'd die rather than see our standards falter or fall.

{=DzyAgYIX}But Pero the second led the last of the real armies into an ambush in the woods outside Zaragoza. You may have heard that story. I was laid up sick at the time. But a lot of my comrades didn't come back, and things were never the same afterward.
{=6OF0vjQV}After Chaime's so-called reforms, they try to maintain an army on the cheap, letting the rich run the show out of their own pocket and we're mocked by the Andalusi we used to rule.
{=0SnKUaex}No doubt a sad state of affairs. So what do you do?
{=WQCAanal}Ah, the old days. And no doubt back then they complained about the older days. I've heard enough.

{=yx5yqDby}Well, I can't just sit around and drink myself to death, so I sell my sword to anyone worth following. I'm a good trainer - I don't bother with horsemen or archers, but if you've got good steady footmen I can do a lot with them. I know a bit about siege machinery too.

{=GLXrkZbW}I'm a soldier of the Crown armies. How we came to this sorry state is a sad tale that you've no doubt heard already from my comrades. But I'm sure you're familiar with our mettle in battle, and our skill at turning youths into soldiers.
{=22ajCymb}Oh, I suppose I can tell you. Nothing that no one doesn't already know, no how.

{=RaqBSakL}Me and some lads from my village, we thought we'd run away, join some other lads up there who make their living on the highways, if you know what I mean. Didn't much want to go scratching a plow across the dry fields all year every year like my father did.

{=xZuqLfE7}But I was young and foolish. Bandits - well, who'd have thought? They're real animals. Always drunk and stabbing each other, or too drunk to feel cold so they catch the plague and end up coughing up blood, or drunk and asleep when the patrol comes around so they end up on the gallows - I suspect you get the idea.
{=qgmt8aPz}So that was no kind of life, neither. I left the woods, paid a little money to friends who got my name off the outlaw list, and now I'm a regular law-abiding citizen. Officially, that is.
{=QIrR9NhL}A wise decision.
{=xzLIIPjB}A knave who could not abide other men's knavery! May such wonders never cease.

{=hinn6pmX}Life's better here in the city. More 'civilized,' you could say. Oh, the work can be a bit rough and tumble, but I feel I can relax after a long day with more refined company, like your good self.

{=suf4hUxN}When I was a lad, I dabbled in crime and banditry a bit, but am now seeking more honest work. I still like the fighting and killing part, though. Anyway, I'm sure you've met a fair amount of men like me on your journeys.
{=56VPnxQd}You're a nosy one, I see.
{=qlAW1aTi}What do you want to know about? My family? Piss on them. I left home as a child and never had cause to regret it. My brothers can take my inheritance, and I hope they choke on it.
{=ikVu2vQU}I've fought in a few wars, for one side or the other. You want to know about my comrades-in-arms? Piss on them too. They all turn on you in the end.
{=iWe4KrSO}About my past, that's all I have to say.
{=AX7gvmaj}So... What are you good at?
{=Aj9ulUly}You're a cheerful one. Good day to you.
{=X9zcXYPa}I can fight. And I don't mind doing work that others would shirk.
{=Ycc0gsX9}I kill for a living. You don't look too wet behind the ears, so I'm sure I don't need to tell you any more about myself than that.
{=VILYhtjW}I'll tell you my story.

{=R9KivNZ6}You may have heard of the Brethren of the Poor — the Waldensians. They preached that riches corrupt, that the Church is lost, and that only a pure heart and an open Bible can lead you to salvation. This was some years ago, in the hills north of Jaca. I followed their call, more for justice than faith. Some of us were believers. Others, like me, just hated the greed of the lords.

{=7JMaPpkN}We lived rough in the forests and upland meadows, speaking only the truth, sharing everything. Then came the edicts — outlawing us, burning books, burning people. So we took up arms. We raided tax caravans and burned tithe barns. Some say we became no better than the lords we hated, taking what we needed with blade and bow.

{=7caHipXX}When the king’s knights finally broke our camps, I fled south. I hear some of my old comrades now guard merchant convoys — or hang in irons in Zaragoza. Me? I keep to myself. Say little. Move quick.
{=wk1jPh1S}A sad tale.
{=OsWmIhds}Heresy, rebellion, and looting are three intertwined serpents. You should bear that brand yourself, knave.

{=HcMbaZn4}I picked up some useful tricks in those days — tracking, hiding, knowing where trouble waits in the hills. Might be I can offer that to a caravan master... or someone like you. For a price.
{=DIaT6pXi}Aye, I once followed a prophet and lived off the land, but that was another life. Now I keep my blade sharp and my head down. You’ve met men like me before, I’m sure.
{=eeEra0bI}Yeah, I'll tell you my story. Pass the wine and listen close.

{=qVQdEYyJ}My family had debts so I joined the army. I guess I always liked men's work more than women's work. The armies took hill women as scouts but I was never the hidey-sneaky type. So I did what I did to be in the spear line.
{=yPRaUTwX}Dressed as a man, of course. Weren't difficult to do. Not hard to fool men. Bound my breasts and watched where I pissed. It was easy.

{=FP9jn3zQ} Well, you know what happened. King Pero the second led the armies to their deaths. I was there. Our detachment didn't see the battle but was part of the rout. That was the end of army life for me. Don't care for glory-hounding lords leading me to my death. But I still like to fight.
{=8CixQpyO}Well, there's always work for the brave and the resourceful.
{=bhvZuX1m}A rather sad tale of deceit and disgrace.

{=TzOdlTeb}Yeah.. The merchants give me jobs. But I'm not sure I care much for guarding caravans. A lot of sitting around listening to boring talk about prices. Look, I can wield a sword or spear and I can also repair them. Wouldn't mind trying my hand them at making them too. If you've know a captain with a good company, a fighting company, maybe he'd be interested.
{=Jv85koU3}I dressed as a man to fight in the campaigns. There are many of us who've done that, believe it or not. You've probably met one of us before, even if you didn't know it.
{=KNCnJN4n}I'll tell you my story. If it bothers you, I'd prefer you keep it to yourself – unless you've lived months on crusts of bread and the leavings of mule trains, that is.

{=pD92hewA}My father was a spice trader in Zaragoza. We lived near the market square in a tall house with balconies and carved doors. Our neighbor was a court notary with friends in the king’s treasury. When the riot came — after a dispute over grain taxes turned bloody — it was not a good street to live on.

{=OGyYFIEA}The mob came to burn the notary's house. His guards fired crossbows into the crowd, and then someone tossed a lamp, and fire swept through the whole quarter. My father ran inside for his coin chest. He never came out. I ran with my mother and brothers to my uncle’s estate, but he feared harboring beggars and turned us away. We followed the refugee trains west, stealing a little, begging a lot.

{=aNSIajhR}I found a soldier slumped drunk near the roadside — still wearing his coat of mail. Maybe he helped torch our quarter, maybe not. But I took his dagger. And then his purse. And then his sword. I still have it.
{=cGr6RvX7}The starving do what they must.
{=iaagSMEa}And to think you had all the advantages of an educated childhood.
{=RZglJSZ3}You learn to be quick, to weigh worth, to smell risk. That’s merchant’s sense — it doesn’t go away, even in rags. I think I’d make myself useful, if you’ve coin and cause enough.
{=QUamabEa}My family dealt in cloth and spices. When the guild riots reached our street, we lost everything. I've lived off trade and grit ever since.
{=ZhZlhNfn}Aye, so you want my story then?

{=Aqbu1o9v}Far back as I remember, I was running wild through the alleys of Valencia with the other barefoot rats. There was a gang down by the old merchant quarter — called themselves *els Corbs*, “the horned ones.” They took a shine to me. First it was messages and picking purses. Then it was knives, slipped quiet into backs. For a crust of bread and a warm cloak, I’d have slit a bishop's throat.

{=imBWFomj}But as I got older, their looks changed. Leering, hands where they shouldn’t be, promises I never asked for. I was just another prize to them. That’s when I started planning my way out — but you don’t just walk away from *els Corbs*.

{=axnObjSY}Then came the Great Fire of Valencia — some say it started in the tanner’s yard, others blame a jealous noble. All I know is half the city was looting and screaming. I slipped into their den, stole a pouch of silver, and vanished into the smoke.
{=LIYZlInR}A hard life, but I must admire your fortitude, at least.
{=haiCZjua}You truly are the dregs of the street, my lady.
{=aULjoDXL}I’ve wandered a while since, but I’ve still got my knives, and I still know how to use them. You need someone watched, followed, or cut — I’ll do it, so long as the pay’s clean.
{=L7tJUtlN}I was raised among criminals in the back alleys of Valencia. I’ve seen the worst of folk, and sometimes been the worst myself. But I’ve got skills. I can help — if the coin’s right.
{=rOUaG9iG}I can tell you my story, though it is painful to recall.

{=vAragMedA}I was trained in medicine at the Estudi General of Lleida. I had hoped to serve God and man through healing. For a time, I served the noble houses of Valencia. I believed myself untouched by politics, merely a servant of health.

{=vAragMedB}But one evening I was approached by a friar in plain robes, bearing the seal of the Aragonese Inquisition. He praised my skill… then asked me to inspect a prisoner, said to be hiding Cathar heresies. At first, I thought they needed a healer. I was wrong.

{=vAragMedC}They demanded I 'assist' in their examination. At first, I refused. But they reminded me of the lives my silence could protect — my patients, my apprentices. The methods they taught me — they haunt me. My instruments became tools not of healing, but agony.
{=BIsvfC0x}You too are a victim of the hideous power of the state.
{=hikXX6zE}You broke your oath. It is not surprising that you fell further.
{=vAragMedD}I left the city. I tried to forget. Opium dulled the memory, but dulled me as well. I wander now, seeking honest coin, and hoping someday to do some small good again.
{=pya1JSNe}I was a surgeon, trained at the Estudi General in Lleida. But events forced me to flee my city, and now I earn my keep however I can. There are many such as I on the road these days.
{=CAiFXWEf}Yes... I can tell you my tale...

{=FybsZ7yK}My father served as a knight in the royal host of the Crown of Aragon. He died on campaign near Valencia. After that, I was placed under the guardianship of a caballero loyal to the court of Lleida. He saw more value in my dowry than in my well-being, and soon arranged a match — to his drunken nephew.

{=omEoa2q7}I would not marry that pig! He stank of wine and bragged of bashing in heads on the battlefield, though I doubt he’d ever held a sword straight. So I did what any daughter of a proper knight would do — I took my father’s warhorse, the one he trained me on, and his blade, and I left before dawn.

{=j7cE9DTZ}They say I stole it — my own horse! Bah. The caballero went to the courts, of course, trying to soil my name and seize the estate outright. Let him have it. I would rather earn my bread with honor than live gilded in a cage.
{=tx6L29wu}You've got spirit, I'll give you that.
{=O3Pkql2y}Takes more to being a warrior than a horse and a sword, young one.

{=UWWgML2g}I may be young, but I know how nobles think — and how horses move. Let me ride with you, learn from you. And one day, perhaps I’ll ride back into Lleida and take back what was mine by right.
{=wRS9yqyx}I left home rather than be wed to a brute. My father was a knight — a cavalryman — and taught me to ride. Now I seek a commander who values a loyal blade more than a dowry.
